1.exposition: The trumpet-interlude reaches its strangest and most contested scene. John is handed a measuring-reed and told to measure the temple, the altar, and its worshippers, but to leave the outer court unmeasured, given over to the nations to trample the holy city for forty-two months. Then two witnesses appear, clothed in sackcloth, to prophesy 1,260 days — the same broken span reckoned three ways across the book. Armed with the fire of Elijah and the plagues of Moses, they cannot be silenced until their testimony is finished; only then does the beast from the abyss kill them, and their bodies lie unburied in the great city where their Lord was crucified. The world gloats and exchanges gifts — until, after three and a half days, the breath of life from God raises them and a voice calls them up to heaven while their enemies watch. An earthquake follows, and the survivors, for once, give glory to the God of heaven. The whole scene is the church's own passion and vindication, told in miniature.
1.original_language: Two Greek words are rendered “temple”: hieron, the whole sacred precinct with its outer courts, and naos, the sanctuary proper — and John is told to measure the naos, the inner house, while the outer court (aulē) is “cast out” (ekbale exō), a forceful verb Barnes and Robertson note is far stronger than “leave out.” The reed is a kalamos “like a rhabdos,” a measuring-rod. The witnesses embody the book's key term martyria (“testimony, witness”), the word that will pass into English as “martyr” along precisely this trajectory — witness borne unto death. And the appointed span is given three ways: forty-two months, 1,260 days, and (in 12:14) a “time, times, and half a time” — a broken seven, three and a half, drawn from Daniel 7:25 and Daniel 12:7: a limited period of tribulation that always falls short of completeness.
1.historical_context: Every image here is quarried from the prophets. The command to measure recalls Ezekiel's visionary surveyor and Zechariah's man with a measuring-line. The two olive trees and lampstands come straight from Zechariah 4, where they are Zerubbabel and Joshua, the anointed civil and priestly leaders who supply the sanctuary's light. The witnesses' powers are those of the two great prophets: shutting the sky like Elijah — whose drought lasted the same three and a half years (Luke 4:25) — and calling down fire (1 Kings 18:38); turning water to blood and smiting with plagues like Moses, the very pair who stood with Christ at the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:3). The three-and-a-half-year measure is itself Daniel's, born of Antiochus IV's desecration of the temple. And “the great city … where their Lord was crucified” (v.8) is a composite symbolic geography — Sodom for its vice, Egypt for its bondage, Jerusalem for its guilt — the world-city wherever it sets itself against God.
1.christ: The witnesses' story is deliberately shaped as the gospel in miniature: prophetic testimony, then death at the hands of the beast, their corpses left in “the great city where their Lord was crucified” (v.8) — their passion set in the same place as his. They lie dead three and a half days, echoing his three days in the tomb, until “the breath of life from God” (v.11) raises them, as it raised the dry bones of Ezekiel 37; then they ascend “in a cloud” while their enemies look on (v.12), the very pattern of the Lord's own ascension (Acts 1:9). The church's vocation is thus grounded in and conformed to Christ's: to witness, to suffer, and to be vindicated exactly as he was.

verses.note: John is handed a reed like a rod and told to measure the temple, the altar, and its worshippers. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson take the measuring-reed from Ezekiel 40 and Zechariah 2: to measure is to mark out, claim, and preserve. But the temple itself divides the readers. grammatical-historical Clarke holds it must be the literal temple at Jerusalem — a hint, he thinks, that it was still standing when John wrote — while grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read it symbolically as the true church, God's dwelling now (1 Corinthians 3:16), measured to be preserved amid danger. The latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede agree on the symbol: the reed is the rule of faith and the gospel, and the measuring marks the true worshippers off from those who are Christian in name alone.

The outer court is left unmeasured, “given to the nations,” who tread the holy city forty-two months — and here the schools part. grammatical-historical Barnes, with the historicist tradition, applies the day-year principle (Daniel 7:25) to read the forty-two months as 1,260 years of the church trodden down by an apostate power; grammatical-historical Clarke and wesleyan-arminian Wesley take it more literally of Jerusalem trodden by Rome and its successors, a “symbolical period” of three and a half years. grammatical-historical Ellicott cautions against fixing it too tightly — forty-two months is the symbol of a limited season, “the pilgrimage period of the Church,” under the world's power yet held within God's hand. reformed Gill reads the unmeasured court as the merely nominal church, a form of godliness without its power.

Christ gives his two witnesses power to prophesy 1,260 days, clothed in sackcloth — and their identity is the chapter's great crux. The older patristic reading, which reformed Gill records, took them as two literal prophets — Enoch and Elijah, or Moses and Elijah — returning to oppose antichrist, and the signs of vv.5-6 lend it color. Gill himself, with the historicist tradition, rejects this for the faithful ministers and churches who witnessed through the long apostasy (the Waldenses especially), and rejects too the view that the two are the Old and New Testaments. puritan Matthew Henry hears in the number two a sufficiency: as Christ sent his disciples out two by two, so “in the mouth of two witnesses” the cause stands — few in evil times, but enough, testifying in the sackcloth of mourning.

“These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.” grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott trace the figure to Zechariah 4: as the olive trees there feed the lampstand with oil, so the witnesses are filled with and channel the oil of the Spirit — their testimony sustained “not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit” (Zechariah 4:6) — and as lampstands they hold forth the light of truth in a dark age, standing in God's own presence.

Fire proceeds from their mouth and devours their enemies. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear Elijah's fire (2 Kings 1:10); grammatical-historical Jamieson, Fausset and Brown read the fire rather as the judicial power of their word — “I will make my words in your mouth fire” (Jeremiah 5:14). Whichever way, the point is inviolability: whoever would harm them “must in this manner be killed.” They cannot be silenced until their commission is done.

Their powers are borrowed from the two prophets of old: to shut the sky so it does not rain, as grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note of Elijah's three-and-a-half-year drought (Luke 4:25), and to turn water to blood and strike the earth with every plague, as Moses did to Egypt (Barnes, Robertson). The witnessing church is not defenseless; its word carries the judicial weight of Moses and Elijah against a persecuting world.

Only “when they have finished their testimony” can they be touched — their inviolability lasts exactly to the end of their commission (grammatical-historical Robertson). Then grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott mark the book's first mention of “the beast that ascends from the abyss,” the great persecuting power later drawn out in chapters 13 and 17 (the antichristian power, to the historicists). It “makes war” on the witnesses, overcomes and kills them: the testimony, for a moment, is silenced and seemingly defeated.

Their corpses lie in the street of the great city — “spiritually called Sodom and Egypt, where also their Lord was crucified.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson feel the indignity of a body left unburied in public scorn. grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the names symbolically — Sodom for its wickedness, Egypt for its oppression — as the apostate persecuting city (Rome, to the historicists). reformed Gill notes that “where their Lord was crucified” most naturally recalls Jerusalem, or any power that crucifies Christ afresh in his members: the witnesses die where their Master died.

Peoples, tribes, tongues, and nations gaze on the bodies three and a half days and refuse them burial. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the fourfold sweep — the whole hostile world looks on — while grammatical-historical Ellicott takes the three and a half days as the short, limited span of the witnesses' apparent death (three and a half years, to the historicists). To deny a grave was the ultimate contempt, the persecutors' triumph over the silenced witnesses.

The earth-dwellers rejoice, make merry, and send gifts to one another, glad to be rid of the prophets. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott recognize the exchange of gifts as a festival custom (Esther 9:22). puritan Matthew Henry names the reason plainly: “these two prophets tormented them” — their faithful witness had vexed the world's conscience, and the world celebrates its relief. It is the high-water mark of the persecutor's joy, and it lasts three and a half days.

Then the reversal: “the breath of life from God entered them, and they stood on their feet.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the dry bones of Ezekiel 37 stirring; reformed Gill compares the breath God breathed into Adam, and reads the raising as the revival of the witnessing church the world thought dead — for historicists, at the Reformation. “Great fear fell on those who saw them”: the world's festival curdles into terror.

A great voice calls, “Come up here,” and they ascend to heaven in a cloud while their enemies watch. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the pattern of Christ's own ascension (Acts 1:9) and Elijah's translation; grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the summons as the call to glory. The vindication is public and complete: the very persecutors who exulted over their death now behold their exaltation.

At that hour a great earthquake topples a tenth of the city, seven thousand are killed, and the survivors give glory to the God of heaven. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the quake as a great convulsion shaking the persecuting power (a political and religious upheaval, to the historicists); grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the definite “seven thousand.” Most striking is the response: the terrified remnant give God glory — the first such turning in the book, in pointed contrast to those who “did not repent” under the earlier trumpets (9:20-21).

“The second woe has passed; behold, the third woe comes quickly.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the structuring note, matching the earlier 9:12: the long sixth trumpet is over. grammatical-historical Ellicott sees it close the whole section of chapters 9-11. puritan Matthew Henry adds the paradox to come: the third woe is the seventh trumpet, and it brings not only the last woe but the arrival of Christ's kingdom — the final judgment and the final triumph bound together.

witnesses.point: The reed is the rule and measure of faith; the measured temple is the confessing church, marked off from those united to it in name only.
The two witnesses are not Enoch and Elijah returned, but the faithful ministers and churches — the Waldenses especially — who testified against antichrist through the long apostasy.
Two is a sufficient number of witnesses: few in evil times, but enough, as Christ sent his disciples out two by two, testifying in the sackcloth of mourning.
The forty-two months is the symbol of a limited season — the Church's pilgrimage period under the world's power — not a datable stretch of years.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: Reads the two witnesses as the whole church in its prophetic vocation to the nations, patterned on Moses and Elijah — and argues that it is their faithful death, not their miraculous powers, that finally moves the world: the survivors who “give glory to God” (v.13) mark a genuine repentance, the harvest of the church's martyr-witness.

1.application: The witnesses are not spared death; they are vindicated through it. For a church tempted to measure faithfulness by safety, chapter 11 holds out a harder and better hope: the testimony that seems silenced in the street is exactly the testimony God raises and lifts to heaven. Witness borne even to the death is never wasted.

15.exposition: The seventh trumpet sounds at last — the third woe — yet what breaks out is not another plague but a burst of praise. Great voices in heaven announce that “the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever.” The long-contested dominion of the world has passed, once and for all, to God and his Anointed. The twenty-four elders fall on their faces and worship, giving thanks that the Almighty “who is and who was” has taken his great power and begun to reign. Their prayer gathers the book's whole hope into a single breath: the raging nations judged, the dead judged, God's servants rewarded, and the destroyers of the earth destroyed. Then heaven's temple is opened and the ark of the covenant appears, amid lightning, thunder, earthquake, and hail — God's covenant faithfulness disclosed in the tokens of his majesty. The trumpet-series ends not in ruin but in a coronation.
15.original_language: The proclamation's verb is decisive: “has become” is an aorist (egeneto), an accomplished fact announced from heaven's side of history even before its earthly unfolding — the kingdom is not merely coming but has come. “Our Lord and … his Christ” echoes Psalm 2, where Yahweh and his Anointed (māshiaḥ / Christos) stand together against the raging nations; yet the reign is one, for “he shall reign” is singular — Father and Son sharing a single throne. The elders' address — “Lord God Almighty, who is and who was” (v.17) — pointedly drops the “who is to come” of 1:4 and 4:8: at the consummation the Coming One has arrived, and the future tense is retired.
15.historical_context: The seventh trumpet is both the third woe and the climax of the whole trumpet cycle, and its “woe” is delivered as a hymn. The twenty-four elders — first seen enthroned in chapter 4 — represent the redeemed people of God, often read as the twelve patriarchs and twelve apostles together. The ark of the covenant they glimpse (v.19) had vanished at the destruction of the first temple in 586 BC; its appearance in the heavenly sanctuary declares that what the earthly temple lost is kept safe with God, his covenant intact. The accompanying signs — lightnings, voices, thunders, an earthquake, and great hail — are the standard tokens of theophany and judgment (Exodus 19; 8:5; 16:18), the same convulsions that attend God's throne throughout the book.
15.christ: “The kingdom … has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ” (v.15) is the goal toward which the whole book strains: the joint and everlasting reign of the Father and the Messiah. The elders' “who is and who was,” shorn of “who is to come” (v.17), quietly confesses that in Christ the future has become present. And the ark disclosed at the close (v.19) is finally a christological sign — it held the tablets of the law, the manna, and Aaron's budded rod, all types of the one who fulfills the law, is the bread of life, and is the true high priest. When the New Jerusalem comes there will be no temple in it, “for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb” (21:22); here that end is already sung.

verses.note: The seventh angel sounds, and heaven's great voices cry that “the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ; and he shall reign forever and ever.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear in the final trumpet not a disaster but the announced consummation — the moment God “reigns” in the sense of openly manifesting the dominion always his. grammatical-historical Ellicott calls it the goal of the whole book proleptically declared. reformed Gill puts it concretely: the kingdoms antichrist had usurped now return to their lawful sovereign, to be governed by his laws forever.

The twenty-four elders, enthroned before God, fall on their faces and worship. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott identify them as the heavenly representatives of the redeemed church first seen in chapter 4 — perhaps the twelve patriarchs and twelve apostles, the whole people of God. puritan Matthew Henry marks the posture: at the announcement of the kingdom heaven answers not with self-congratulation but with the lowliest adoration, faces to the ground before God.

Their thanksgiving: “We give you thanks, Lord God Almighty, who is and who was, for you have taken your great power and begun to reign.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the title carried over from 1:4 and 4:8, now with a telling omission: the “who is to come” is dropped, for — as the grammatical-historical expositors observe — God is no longer the Coming One but the one who has come and reigns. grammatical-historical Ellicott: the kingdom so long awaited is now openly established.

“The nations raged, but your wrath has come” — grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear Psalm 2: the fury of the nations is met by the settled wrath of God. The seventh trumpet then gathers the whole end into one clause — the time to judge the dead, to reward “your servants the prophets and saints, small and great” (Barnes, Ellicott), and, as puritan Matthew Henry stresses, “to destroy the destroyers of the earth.” Judgment and reward, ruin and recompense, arrive together with the kingdom.

The chapter closes as “God's temple in heaven was opened, and the ark of his covenant was seen,” with lightnings, voices, thunders, an earthquake, and great hail. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the opened sanctuary and the long-hidden ark as the disclosure of God's covenant presence and faithfulness; grammatical-historical Ellicott takes the ark as the pledge of promises kept for his people. The attending signs (cf. Exodus 19) are the tokens of divine majesty and judgment — closing the trumpets and opening the great signs of the woman and the dragon that follow.

witnesses.point: The kingdoms of the world antichrist had usurped now return to their lawful sovereign, to be subject to Christ and governed by his laws forever.
The seventh trumpet is the grand consummation — the time when God will “reign” by more fully manifesting his dominion and establishing a kingdom of righteousness.
The elders answer the kingdom's arrival with prostrate worship, thanking God that judgment, reward, and the destruction of the destroyers all come with his reign.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: The seventh trumpet narrates no new plague but announces the end already achieved from heaven's vantage; the aorist “has become” declares the kingdom an accomplished fact, and on a recapitulatory reading it reaches the same consummation the bowls will reach again from another angle.

15.application: The seventh trumpet lets the suffering church hear the end of its own story: the kingdom of the world has already become the kingdom of its Lord. Endurance, then, is not a gamble on an uncertain outcome but a response to a victory already announced from the throne. We still wait — but we wait as those who have been told how it ends.

_meta.title: The Kingdom of the World Become the Kingdom of Our Lord
reflection: The two witnesses prophesy, are killed, lie unburied, and are raised — the church's story patterned on its Lord's (vv.3-12). Where is God calling you to a witness whose vindication may lie on the far side of loss?
Their bodies lie “where their Lord was crucified” (v.8): the church suffers where its Master suffered. How does sharing Christ's pattern of rejection reframe the cost of your own faithfulness?

At the seventh trumpet heaven announces that “the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ” (v.15) as an accomplished fact. How would living under an already-declared victory change the way you endure this week?
